justification: All 5 expected rules identified (BR-1 through BR-4 as invariant: true, BR-6 weight bracket as invariant: false). Agent went beyond by splitting weight bounds into BR-4 and BR-5, correctly marking the 1000kg cap as invariant: false (defensible interpretation). State machine directionality explicitly modeled with in_transit -> pending forbidden (BR-2) and delivered terminal (BR-3). All 12 acceptance criteria use Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable. Confidence level at 72 with clearly stated assumptions. Weight bracket correctly marked invariant: false with sound justification.

observations.text: Exceeded minimum rule count by splitting weight validation into physical constraint (BR-4, invariant: true) and business policy (BR-5, invariant: false) — a nuanced and defensible domain modeling choice.
State machine directionality is explicit and thorough — both backward transition (In Transit → Pending) and terminal state (Delivered → any) are independently modeled.
Testable assertions are highly concrete with specific boundary values (e.g., '10 kg is Standard, 10.01 kg is Heavy').
Identified two meaningful escalations (skip transitions, weight mutability) that demonstrate genuine domain reasoning beyond the spec.
Edge cases cover boundary values, near-zero weights, identical addresses, and post-creation mutability — thorough coverage.
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated with clearly articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions.

BR-5 marking the 1000kg upper bound as invariant: false is debatable — the rubric expects weight > 0 and <= 1000 as a single invariant: true rule. However, the justification is reasonable (business policy vs physical constraint).
